questions.stem: A ventilated patient has a PA catheter. Current readings: PCWP 6 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, CO 2.4 L/min, SVR 1,840 dyn·s/cm⁵, MAP 52 mmHg. Which hemodynamic profile and etiology is MOST consistent?

options.text: Cardiogenic shock: low CO, high PCWP, high SVR
Distributive (septic) shock: low CO, low PCWP, low SVR
Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, high SVR
Obstructive shock: low CO, elevated PCWP, elevated CVP

questions.rationale: Hypovolemic shock profile: low CO (inadequate preload), low PCWP (depleted LV filling), low CVP (low venous return), HIGH SVR (compensatory vasoconstriction to maintain pressure). Distributive/septic shock (B) also has low PCWP but will have LOW SVR (massive vasodilation). Cardiogenic shock (A) has low CO with HIGH PCWP (backed-up fluid). Obstructive shock (D) typically has elevated CVP with equalization of filling pressures. The combination of low CO + low PCWP + HIGH SVR + low MAP = hypovolemia.

distractorRationales.a: Cardiogenic shock: low CO, high PCWP, high SVR misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals shock points to Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, high SVR, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.b: Distributive (septic) shock: low CO, low PCWP, low SVR misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals shock points to Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, high SVR, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Obstructive shock: low CO, elevated PCWP, elevated CVP misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals shock points to Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, high SVR, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, HIGH SVR. Distributive: low CO, low PCWP, LOW SVR. Cardiogenic: low CO, HIGH PCWP, high SVR.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An 85-year-old with moderate Alzheimer's dementia becomes extremely agitated, combative, and confused every evening around 5-6 PM (sundowning behavior). She repeatedly attempts to climb out of bed and is at high fall risk. Her family requests soft wrist restraints. Which nursing approach is MOST appropriate as first-line management?

options.text: Apply soft wrist restraints bilaterally — this is the safest approach to prevent falls during sundowning
Administer haloperidol 5 mg IV as a PRN order for agitation episodes

Implement non-pharmacological interventions: increased daytime activity and light exposure, consistent routine, family presence during peak agitation time, reduced evening stimulation, and calm redirection
Request a standing order for lorazepam 1 mg PO nightly as a preventive measure against evening agitation

questions.rationale: Physical restraints in elderly dementia patients with sundowning WORSEN outcomes: they increase agitation and distress, increase fall and injury risk (patients attempt to escape restraints), contribute to delirium, cause pressure injuries, aspiration, and death. Restraints are a last resort, never first-line, and require physician order with regular reassessment. Evidence-based non-pharmacological first-line approaches: (1) Light therapy — bright light exposure in the morning resets circadian rhythm; reduce stimulation in the evening; (2) Consistent daily routine and familiar environment; (3) Family presence during peak agitation times (5-7 PM); (4) Structured meaningful activity during the day prevents excessive daytime sleeping; (5) Calm, simple, one-step verbal redirection. If pharmacotherapy is needed after non-pharmacological failure, low-dose scheduled quetiapine may be considered. High-dose IV haloperidol is not appropriate first-line. Lorazepam and all benzodiazepines are explicitly Beers Criteria-inappropriate in elderly dementia patients — they worsen confusion and fall risk.

distractorRationales.a: Apply soft wrist restraints bilaterally — this is the safest approach to prevent falls during sundowning misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology aspiration points to Implement non-pharmacological interventions: increased daytime activity and light exposure, consistent routine, family presence during peak agitation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.b: Administer haloperidol 5 mg IV as a PRN order for agitation episodes misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology aspiration points to Implement non-pharmacological interventions: increased daytime activity and light exposure, consistent routine, family presence during peak agitation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Request a standing order for lorazepam 1 mg PO nightly as a preventive measure against evening misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology aspiration points to Implement non-pharmacological interventions: increased daytime activity and light exposure, consistent routine, family presence during peak agitation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Sundowning first-line: non-pharmacological — light therapy, routine, family presence, daytime activity, redirection. Physical restraints WORSEN agitation and cause harm. Benzodiazepines contraindicated in elderly dementia patients.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a 7-year-old child notices multiple bruises in various stages of healing on the torso, bilateral arm bruises with patterned marks, and the child flinches when the nurse approaches. The mother explains the bruises are from 'falling off a bike.' Which nursing action is correct?

options.text: Document the mother's explanation in the chart and continue routine care
Confront the mother and ask her directly if she is abusing the child
Report suspicion of child abuse to the appropriate state agency regardless of whether the nurse is certain
Discuss concerns with the healthcare team and only report if the physician agrees the findings are suspicious

questions.rationale: Nurses are mandatory reporters in all 50 states. Mandatory reporting requires reporting SUSPICION of child abuse — absolute certainty is not required. A nurse who fails to report suspected child abuse can face criminal charges, license sanctions, and civil liability. The threshold is reasonable suspicion based on assessment findings. The report goes directly to the state's child protective services (CPS) — this is the nurse's independent legal obligation, not contingent on physician agreement (D). Confronting the caregiver (B) may endanger the child. Documentation alone (A) does not fulfill the mandatory report obligation.

distractorRationales.a: Document the mother's explanation in the chart and continue routine care misses the clinical pattern: Mandatory Reporting assessment points to Report suspicion of child abuse to the appropriate state agency regardless of whether the nurse is, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen pediatric or newborn instability.
distractorRationales.b: Confront the mother and ask her directly if she is abusing the child misses the clinical pattern: Mandatory Reporting assessment points to Report suspicion of child abuse to the appropriate state agency regardless of whether the nurse is, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen pediatric or newborn instability.
distractorRationales.d: Discuss concerns with the healthcare team and only report if the physician agrees the findings are misses the clinical pattern: Mandatory Reporting assessment points to Report suspicion of child abuse to the appropriate state agency regardless of whether the nurse is, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen pediatric or newborn instability.

questions.takeaway: Mandatory reporting: report SUSPICION of abuse — you do not need to be certain; report directly to CPS; this is the nurse's legal obligation regardless of physician agreement.

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing two patients. Patient A has BP 80/50, sodium 124 mEq/L, potassium 6.1 mEq/L, and hyperpigmentation. Patient B has BP 158/94, sodium 148 mEq/L, potassium 3.1 mEq/L, and truncal obesity with purple striae. Which conditions CORRECTLY match these presentations?

options.text: Patient A: Cushing's syndrome; Patient B: Addison's disease
Patient A: Addison's disease (adrenal insufficiency); Patient B: Cushing's syndrome
Patient A: Hypothyroidism; Patient B: Hyperaldosteronism
Patient A: SIADH; Patient B: Diabetes insipidus

questions.rationale: Addison's disease (primary adrenal insufficiency): cortisol and aldosterone deficiency → hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypotension, and hyperpigmentation (elevated ACTH stimulates melanocytes). Cushing's syndrome (cortisol excess): hypertension, hypokalemia, hypernatremia, central obesity, purple striae, and moon face from chronic cortisol excess. These are opposite presentations driven by opposite hormonal states.

distractorRationales.a: Patient A: Cushing's syndrome; Patient B: Addison's disease misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced potassium points to Patient A: Addison's disease (adrenal insufficiency); Patient B: Cushing's syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: Patient A: Hypothyroidism; Patient B: Hyperaldosteronism misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced potassium points to Patient A: Addison's disease (adrenal insufficiency); Patient B: Cushing's syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: Patient A: SIADH; Patient B: Diabetes insipidus misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced potassium points to Patient A: Addison's disease (adrenal insufficiency); Patient B: Cushing's syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Addison's = hypoNa, hyperK, hypotension, hyperpigmentation. Cushing's = hyperNa, hypoK, hypertension, truncal obesity, striae.

questions.stem: A patient on clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia develops fever 39.8°C, diaphoresis, severe muscle rigidity ('lead pipe'), altered mental status, and autonomic instability (fluctuating BP, tachycardia). Which condition is this, and what is the immediate action?

options.text: Serotonin syndrome; administer cyproheptadine and stop all serotonergic agents
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS); discontinue antipsychotic immediately and initiate supportive care with dantrolene or bromocriptine
Acute dystonia; administer IV diphenhydramine 50 mg
Anticholinergic toxicity from clozapine; administer physostigmine

questions.rationale: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is a life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction to antipsychotics characterized by the tetrad: hyperthermia, severe muscle rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability. It develops over days-weeks. Immediate management: stop the antipsychotic, aggressive cooling, IV fluids, dantrolene (muscle relaxant) and/or bromocriptine (dopamine agonist). Serotonin syndrome (A) has similar features but presents with hyperreflexia and clonus (not lead-pipe rigidity) and requires cyproheptadine. Acute dystonia (C) does not include fever or autonomic instability.

distractorRationales.a: Serotonin syndrome; administer cyproheptadine and stop all serotonergic agents misses the clinical pattern: Antipsychotic Medication Safety reaction points to Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS); discontinue antipsychotic immediately and initiate supportive care with dantrolene or bromocriptine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Acute dystonia; administer IV diphenhydramine 50 mg misses the clinical pattern: Antipsychotic Medication Safety reaction points to Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS); discontinue antipsychotic immediately and initiate supportive care with dantrolene or bromocriptine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Anticholinergic toxicity from clozapine; administer physostigmine misses the clinical pattern: Antipsychotic Medication Safety reaction points to Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS); discontinue antipsychotic immediately and initiate supportive care with dantrolene or bromocriptine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: NMS tetrad: hyperthermia + lead-pipe rigidity + altered mental status + autonomic instability — stop antipsychotic immediately; treat with dantrolene and/or bromocriptine.

questions.stem: Parents bring their 9-month-old to the ED with episodic, inconsolable crying every 15–20 minutes, pulling the knees to the chest, and passage of stools described as 'jelly-like and dark red.' The abdomen is mildly distended. What is the priority nursing action?

options.text: Administer oral rehydration solution and observe for 2 hours
Prepare the family for a possible emergent surgical laparotomy
Notify the physician; these findings are consistent with intussusception — prepare for diagnostic and potentially therapeutic air or contrast enema
Obtain stool culture for Campylobacter and Salmonella

questions.rationale: Classic intussusception triad: episodic colicky pain (bowel telescoping and releasing), vomiting, and currant jelly stools (blood and mucus from mucosal ischemia). Most common site: ileocecal junction. Peak age: 6–36 months. Diagnosis: ultrasound (target sign). Treatment: air or hydrostatic (contrast) enema under fluoroscopy or ultrasound — diagnostic and therapeutic in ~80% of cases. Surgical reduction is required if enema fails or peritonitis present. Stool culture addresses infectious causes, not mechanical obstruction. Waiting worsens intestinal ischemia.

distractorRationales.a: Administer oral rehydration solution and observe for 2 hours misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing pain points to Notify the physician; these findings are consistent with intussusception — prepare for diagnostic and potentially therapeutic, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.b: Prepare the family for a possible emergent surgical laparotomy misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing pain points to Notify the physician; these findings are consistent with intussusception — prepare for diagnostic and potentially therapeutic, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Obtain stool culture for Campylobacter and Salmonella misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing pain points to Notify the physician; these findings are consistent with intussusception — prepare for diagnostic and potentially therapeutic, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Intussusception: episodic colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stools in infant/toddler → ultrasound diagnosis; air/contrast enema is therapeutic in 80%; surgical if enema fails.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Intussusception: episodic colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stools in infant/toddler → ultrasound diagnosis; air/contrast enema is therapeutic in 80%; surgical if enema fails.
visualRationale.conclusion: Intussusception: episodic colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stools in infant/toddler → ultrasound diagnosis; air/contrast enema is therapeutic in 80%; surgical if enema fails.

questions.stem: A morbidly obese patient with symptomatic GERD requires urgent surgery. The anesthesiologist plans rapid sequence induction (RSI). During RSI, which nursing action is most important to reduce pulmonary aspiration risk?

options.text: Place the patient in Trendelenburg position to maximize airway visualization
Apply firm cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver) as directed by the anesthesia provider until ETT placement is confirmed
Insert a nasogastric tube immediately before induction to fully decompress the stomach
Administer metoclopramide IV push 10 minutes before induction to empty the stomach

questions.rationale: RSI is used for patients at elevated aspiration risk (obesity, GERD, full stomach, pregnancy, bowel obstruction). It combines: pre-oxygenation → simultaneous administration of a rapid induction agent (propofol/ketamine) and a fast-onset neuromuscular blocking agent (succinylcholine or high-dose rocuronium) → immediate intubation without manual ventilation. The nurse's critical role: apply firm downward pressure on the cricoid cartilage (Sellick maneuver) to compress the esophagus between the cricoid ring and the cervical vertebra, preventing passive regurgitation of gastric contents until ETT cuff is inflated and placement confirmed by capnography. If active vomiting occurs, cricoid pressure must be released immediately (holds vomiting in and increases aspiration risk). NGT cannot adequately empty the stomach in time and does not protect the airway. Trendelenburg (head-down) increases aspiration risk. Metoclopramide is adjunctive preparation, not a substitute for RSI or cricoid pressure.

distractorRationales.a: Place the patient in Trendelenburg position to maximize airway visualization misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care airway points to Apply firm cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver) as directed by the anesthesia provider until ETT placement is, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Insert a nasogastric tube immediately before induction to fully decompress the stomach misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care airway points to Apply firm cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver) as directed by the anesthesia provider until ETT placement is, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Administer metoclopramide IV push 10 minutes before induction to empty the stomach misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care airway points to Apply firm cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver) as directed by the anesthesia provider until ETT placement is, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: RSI aspiration prevention: nurse applies cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver) — compresses esophagus to prevent passive regurgitation during intubation. Release if active vomiting occurs. Used for all high-risk aspiration patients.

questions.stem: A nurse on a step-down unit is monitoring four patients. Which clinical finding requires the nurse to call the rapid response team (RRT) IMMEDIATELY?

options.text: A 60-year-old with pneumonia whose respiratory rate has increased from 18 to 26 breaths/min over the last 2 hours and is now using accessory muscles
A 74-year-old with atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 88 bpm, unchanged from admission
A 55-year-old post-cholecystectomy who reports nausea and has vomited once
A 66-year-old with CHF whose morning weight is 2 lbs higher than yesterday

questions.rationale: A rising respiratory rate to 26/min with accessory muscle use in a pneumonia patient signals deteriorating respiratory status and impending respiratory failure—a classic RRT trigger. Tachypnea > 25/min, increased work of breathing, and a worsening trend over 2 hours meets standard early warning score thresholds for team activation. The AFib patient is at a controlled, unchanged rate (not a new emergency). Post-op nausea with one emesis is common and manageable on the unit. A 2-lb overnight weight gain in CHF warrants provider notification but not RRT activation unless accompanied by respiratory distress.

distractorRationales.b: A 74-year-old with atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 88 bpm, unchanged from admission misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization breathing points to A 60-year-old with pneumonia whose respiratory rate has increased from 18 to 26 breaths/min over the, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: A 55-year-old post-cholecystectomy who reports nausea and has vomited once misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization breathing points to A 60-year-old with pneumonia whose respiratory rate has increased from 18 to 26 breaths/min over the, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: A 66-year-old with CHF whose morning weight is 2 lbs higher than yesterday misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization breathing points to A 60-year-old with pneumonia whose respiratory rate has increased from 18 to 26 breaths/min over the, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: RRT triggers include: RR > 25, acute accessory muscle use, O2 sat drop, acute altered mental status, SBP < 90. Trends matter as much as absolute values—worsening over 2 hours is a red flag.

questions.stem: An RN realizes she forgot to document a medication administered at 0900 and is now documenting at 1400. How should this be documented correctly?

options.text: Backdate the entry to 0900 to reflect the actual time of administration
Write the current time (1400), note 'late entry,' document the actual time of administration (0900), and record the medication and patient response
Use correction fluid (Wite-Out) to correct the medication administration record if paper-based
Skip documenting the medication since it was already administered and observed to have been effective

questions.rationale: Late entry documentation: write the current date and time, clearly label as 'late entry,' include the actual time the event occurred, and document the relevant information. This maintains an accurate legal and clinical record without misrepresentation. Backdating (A) is falsification of medical records — a criminal offense and grounds for license revocation. Correction fluid (C) is never used in medical records; errors are corrected by drawing a single line through the error, initialing, dating, and noting 'error' — never obliterating. Omitting documentation (D) creates a legal and clinical record gap.

distractorRationales.a: Backdate the entry to 0900 to reflect the actual time of administration misses the clinical pattern: Documentation fluid points to Write the current time (1400), note 'late entry,' document the actual time of administration (0900), and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.c: Use correction fluid (Wite-Out) to correct the medication administration record if paper-based misses the clinical pattern: Documentation fluid points to Write the current time (1400), note 'late entry,' document the actual time of administration (0900), and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Skip documenting the medication since it was already administered and observed to have been effective misses the clinical pattern: Documentation fluid points to Write the current time (1400), note 'late entry,' document the actual time of administration (0900), and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Late entry: write current time, label 'late entry,' include actual event time — backdating is falsification; never use Wite-Out on medical records.

questions.stem: A patient on lithium for bipolar disorder presents with tremor, ataxia, slurred speech, and confusion. Serum lithium level is 2.4 mEq/L. Which intervention is the priority?

options.text: Hold the next lithium dose and recheck level in 12 hours
Administer activated charcoal to reduce absorption
Discontinue lithium, provide IV normal saline hydration, and prepare for dialysis evaluation
Administer sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine and enhance lithium excretion

questions.rationale: Lithium toxicity: therapeutic range 0.6–1.2 mEq/L. Level 1.5–2.0 = mild toxicity (nausea, tremor, polyuria). Level 2.0–2.5 = moderate toxicity (ataxia, confusion, slurred speech — this patient). Level >2.5 = severe (seizures, coma, cardiac arrest). Moderate-severe toxicity requires: stop lithium, IV NS hydration (promotes renal lithium excretion), and consideration for hemodialysis (most effective removal method). Simply holding a dose (A) is inadequate. Activated charcoal (B) does not bind lithium effectively. Sodium bicarbonate (D) is used for salicylate toxicity, not lithium.

distractorRationales.a: Hold the next lithium dose and recheck level in 12 hours misses the clinical pattern: Lithium Toxicity sodium points to Discontinue lithium, provide IV normal saline hydration, and prepare for dialysis evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.b: Administer activated charcoal to reduce absorption misses the clinical pattern: Lithium Toxicity sodium points to Discontinue lithium, provide IV normal saline hydration, and prepare for dialysis evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Administer sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine and enhance lithium excretion misses the clinical pattern: Lithium Toxicity sodium points to Discontinue lithium, provide IV normal saline hydration, and prepare for dialysis evaluation, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Lithium level >2.0 mEq/L with neurological symptoms: stop lithium, IV NS hydration, evaluate for hemodialysis.
